input_path: data\papers\The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin (PT)** system under study is a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)-based incubator** designed for temperature regulation within an insulated container. The system operates in the **thermal control domain**, with the primary objective of maintaining a predefined temperature inside a styrofoam box regardless of its internal contents. Key components include:
  - **Insulated Container**: A styrofoam box providing thermal insulation to minimize external environmental influences.
  - **Heating Element**: A heatbed responsible for generating and distributing heat inside the container.
  - **Air Circulation**: A fan to ensure uniform temperature distribution within the box.
  - **Sensors**:
    - Two **internal temperature sensors** for real-time monitoring of the box's internal temperature.
    - One **external temperature sensor** to measure ambient conditions outside the box.
  - **Control Unit**: A **Raspberry Pi** acting as the central controller for the system, interfacing with sensors and actuators (e.g., heatbed) to regulate temperature dynamically. The system is representative of **CPS** due to its integration of physical processes (thermal dynamics) with computational logic (control algorithms), making it suitable for **Digital Twin (DT) implementation** to enhance predictability, safety, and adaptability to environmental changes.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** of the incubator provides the following **services** to users and the physical system, leveraging **modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimization** technologies:
  - **Real-Time Monitoring**: Continuous collection of **sensor data** (internal/external temperatures) from the **Physical Twin (PT)** to track operational state and detect deviations.
  - **Dynamic Simulation**: Execution of **calibrated models** (e.g., differential equations for thermal dynamics) to **reproduce and predict** the PT's behavior under current or hypothetical operating conditions, enabling **what-if analysis**.
  - **Fault Detection & Safety Assurance**: Integration of **monitoring algorithms** to identify **safety violations** (e.g., overheating, sensor failures) and trigger corrective actions (e.g., adjusting heatbed power or alerting operators).
  - **Optimization & Control**: Use of **supervisory controllers** and **state estimators** to optimize temperature regulation, minimize energy consumption, and adapt to **changing environmental conditions** (e.g., external temperature fluctuations).
  - **Predictive Maintenance**: Proactive identification of **wear-and-tear patterns** or **component degradation** (e.g., sensor drift, heatbed inefficiency) through **data-driven algorithms** and model updates.
  - **Human-in-the-Loop Decision Support**: Presentation of **actionable insights** (e.g., calibration recommendations, failure alerts) to human operators or automated systems for **reconfiguration decisions** (e.g., adjusting PID controller parameters).
  - **Advanced Visualization**: Generation of **interactive visualizations** (e.g., temperature gradients, historical trends) to facilitate **engineering communication** and **operational transparency**.
  Target users include **control engineers, maintenance technicians, and system designers**, with services tailored for **safety-critical applications** in environments requiring precise thermal regulation (e.g., medical, agricultural, or industrial processes).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** implementation for the incubator leverages the following **tools, technologies, and enablers**, as inferred from the provided context and case study:
  - **Hardware Platform**:
    - **Raspberry Pi**: Acts as the **embedded controller** for the PT, interfacing with sensors (e.g., DS18B20 temperature sensors) and actuators (e.g., heatbed, fan).
    - **Sensor Network**: Includes **two internal temperature sensors** and **one external sensor** for real-time data acquisition, with specifications available in the [public repository](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator).
  - **Software & Development Tools**:
    - **Modeling Frameworks**: Use of **differential equation-based models** (e.g., for thermal dynamics) to represent the PT's physics, with **calibration algorithms** (e.g., least-squares optimization) to refine model parameters using sensor data.
    - **Simulation Engines**: Execution of **high-fidelity simulations** (e.g., via MATLAB/Simulink or Python-based tools like SciPy) to validate model accuracy and predict system behavior under varying conditions.
    - **Control Algorithms**: Implementation of **supervisory controllers** (e.g., PID, MPC) and **state estimators** (e.g., Kalman filters) for real-time temperature regulation and fault detection.
    - **Data Management**:
      - **Time-Series Databases** (e.g., InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) for storing **sensor telemetry** and historical logs.
      - **Cloud/Edge Hybrid Architecture**: Potential deployment of **edge computing** (e.g., on the Raspberry Pi) for low-latency processing, with cloud-based services for **scalable analytics** and **model training**.
  - **Specialized Technologies**:
    - **Calibration Methods**: **Model calibration techniques** (e.g., Bayesian inference, gradient descent) to align simulated models with real-world PT behavior, as demonstrated in the incubator case study (Section 3).
    - **Digital Twin Framework**: Integration of **modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimization** into a unified framework (as depicted in **Figure 1**), enabling **closed-loop feedback** between the DT and PT.
    - **Open-Source Ecosystem**: Leveraging the **INTO-CPS Association’s example incubator repository** ([GitHub](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)) for hardware/software specifications, experimental validation, and reproducibility.
  - **Validation & Experimentation**:
    - **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) Testing**: Use of **real-time simulation environments** to validate DT models against the PT’s physical responses.
    - **Benchmarking**: Comparison of **model accuracy vs. simulation speed** (noting the trade-off between abstraction levels and computational efficiency, as mentioned in Document 1).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin (DT) system operates with a **real-time synchronization paradigm**, where data from the Physical Twin (PT) is continuously collected via sensors (e.g., temperature sensors in the incubator case study) and fed into the DT framework. The temporal granularity depends on the **sampling rate of the sensors** and the **latency requirements of the calibration algorithms** used to update the models of the Cyber-Physical System (CPS) and its environment. For instance, in the incubator example, temperature sensors provide **continuous monitoring**, implying a high-frequency data acquisition loop (e.g., sub-second or second-level granularity) to ensure accurate model calibration and real-time monitoring of system performance. The DT employs **calibration algorithms** that dynamically adjust model parameters based on the latest sensor data, ensuring the digital representation remains synchronized with the PT's state. Latency requirements are implicitly tied to the **control loop of the CPS**, where delays in data processing or model updates could degrade system responsiveness (e.g., in temperature regulation). The DT’s time scale is thus **event-driven and continuous**, with critical dependencies on the **speed of data acquisition, model update frequency, and algorithmic processing time** to maintain predictive and operational fidelity.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin (DT) architecture supports **multiple instances and hierarchical structures** to manage complexity and scalability. In the provided context, the DT is described as a **centralized framework** integrating models, data, and algorithms for a single Physical Twin (PT), such as the incubator system. However, the broader DT concept implies potential **decentralized or federated architectures** where multiple DT instances could coexist—each representing different aspects (e.g., cyber, physical, environmental) or subsystems of the PT. For example, the incubator DT could include **specialized sub-models** (e.g., thermal dynamics, control logic) managed independently but coordinated under a unified DT orchestration layer. Coordination mechanisms are not explicitly detailed in the documents, but the DT’s **calibration algorithms** and **monitoring services** suggest a **hierarchical or modular design**, where lower-level instances (e.g., sensor data processors) feed into higher-level aggregators (e.g., fault detectors or optimizers). The scope of responsibilities is divided between **data collection (sensors)**, **model management (calibration algorithms)**, and **decision-making (monitors/controllers)**, with potential for **distributed execution** if the system scales beyond a single PT.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin (DT) system follows a **service-oriented architecture (SOA) pattern**, where key components—**data acquisition, models, and algorithms**—are integrated into a cohesive framework to enable real-time interaction with the Physical Twin (PT). The orchestration is centered around a **closed-loop control structure** (as depicted in Figure 1), where:
  1. **Data** from the PT (e.g., sensor readings) is ingested and preprocessed.
  2. **Models** (e.g., differential equations for dynamics, thermal models) are calibrated using algorithms to align with the PT’s current state.
  3. **Monitors** (e.g., safety/performance checkers) evaluate the system’s behavior, triggering **corrective actions** (e.g., controller adjustments) via human or automated intervention.
  The architecture emphasizes **modularity and reconfigurability**, allowing the DT to adapt to changes in the PT or its environment. Resource management is implicit, with **calibration algorithms** dynamically optimizing model parameters and **monitors** allocating computational resources to critical tasks (e.g., fault detection). The DT’s coordination mechanisms rely on **feedback loops** between the cyber (digital) and physical components, ensuring alignment through continuous data-driven updates. The constellation is **tightly coupled** for real-time applications (e.g., the incubator’s temperature control) but could extend to **loosely coupled** configurations for broader system-of-systems integration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) integrates **computational models** and **data components** to represent the Cyber-Physical System (CPS) and its environment. **Model types** include:
  - **Geometric/Structural Models**: Abstract representations of physical components (e.g., CAD models of a car’s chassis, wheels, or an incubator’s insulated box), capturing static attributes like dimensions, shapes, and material properties.
  - **Behavioral/Dynamic Models**: Described by differential equations (e.g., thermal models for the incubator’s heatbed) to simulate physics-based interactions (e.g., temperature dynamics, forces acting on the car). The incubator case study employed **two simulation models**—one with **two free parameters** and another with **four free parameters**—to predict internal temperature, with the latter demonstrating superior accuracy in modeling thermal inertia.
  - **Analytical Models**: Used for optimization, fault detection, and state estimation (e.g., supervisory controllers, optimizers) to manipulate data and models for predictive or corrective actions.

  **Data sources** originate from the **Physical Twin (PT)** via **sensors** (e.g., temperature sensors in the incubator, force/position sensors in a car), collecting real-time operational data (e.g., environmental conditions, system states). **Data management** involves:
  - **Calibration algorithms** to update CPS and environment models with parameter estimates derived from sensor data.
  - **Monitoring systems** that validate model predictions against real-world performance (e.g., checking for safe/optimal operation deviations).
  - **Feedback loops** where detected violations trigger human or algorithmic interventions (e.g., adjusting actuators or recalibrating models).

  **Model relationships** are hierarchical: geometric models provide structural context, while behavioral models simulate dynamic interactions. Data flows from sensors → calibration algorithms → updated models → monitors → decision-making layers. The incubator’s open-source hardware/software stack enables verification of these relationships through empirical validation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The **sensing infrastructure** in the Digital Twin (DT) system is critical for real-time data acquisition from the **Physical Twin (PT)**. While the documents do not specify exact sensor types or spatial distributions, they imply the following technical requirements based on the incubator case study and general DT frameworks:
  - **Sensor Types**: Likely include **temperature sensors** (for the incubator’s internal/external thermal monitoring), **environmental sensors** (e.g., humidity, ambient temperature), and **process sensors** (e.g., heatbed current/voltage for the incubator or ECU-based sensors in a car for dynamics).
  - **Measurement Capabilities**: Focus on **thermal properties** (e.g., heat transfer coefficients, thermal inertia) and **dynamic interactions** (e.g., forces, vibrations in a car). The incubator’s **four-parameter model** suggests high-resolution sensing of transient thermal behaviors.
  - **Sampling Frequencies/Data Transmission Rates**: Not explicitly stated, but implied to be **high-frequency** to capture real-time dynamics (e.g., PID control loops in the incubator or millisecond-scale ECU communications in a car).
  - **Accuracy/Precision**: Critical for model validation; the incubator study highlights the need for sensors capable of resolving **thermal inertia** (a second-order dynamic property), implying **high-precision** (±0.1°C or better for temperature).
  - **Data Collection Mechanisms**: Integrated with the **cyber component** (e.g., embedded controllers, communication networks like CAN in cars or Wi-Fi/Modbus in the incubator) to transmit raw data to the DT for calibration and monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The **fidelity** of Digital Twin (DT) models and data is governed by:
  - **Model Fidelity Requirements**:
    - **Parameterization**: Models must include sufficient **free parameters** to capture critical dynamics (e.g., the incubator’s **four-parameter thermal model** outperformed a two-parameter version by better representing thermal inertia).
    - **Physics-Based Accuracy**: Models must align with **differential equations** (e.g., for temperature dynamics or car dynamics) to ensure predictive validity.
    - **Environmental Context**: Models must account for **uncontrolled environmental factors** (e.g., wind/road conditions for a car, ambient temperature for the incubator).
  - **Validation and Verification Methods**:
    - **Empirical Comparison**: DT predictions are validated against **real-world sensor data** (e.g., incubator temperature measurements vs. model outputs).
    - **Calibration Algorithms**: Continuously adjust model parameters using **sensor feedback** to minimize prediction errors (e.g., updating thermal coefficients in the incubator model).
  - **Uncertainty Sources and Quantification**:
    - **Sensor Noise**: May introduce errors in data used for calibration.
    - **Model Simplifications**: Assumptions (e.g., linear thermal behavior) may deviate from reality.
    - **Environmental Variability**: Unmodeled factors (e.g., sudden wind gusts for a car) can reduce fidelity.
  - **Error Detection and Correction Mechanisms**:
    - **Monitors**: Actively check for deviations between DT predictions and PT performance (e.g., detecting if the incubator’s temperature drifts beyond safe thresholds).
    - **Feedback Loops**: Trigger **recalibration** or **corrective actions** (e.g., adjusting the heatbed’s power or recalibrating the car’s dynamic model).
  - **Quality Assurance Processes**:
    - **Open-Source Validation**: The incubator case study emphasizes **transparent, reproducible experiments** to verify DT techniques (e.g., comparing model predictions to hardware measurements).
    - **Iterative Refinement**: Models are refined based on **operational data** and **expert knowledge** (e.g., engineers tuning the car’s dynamic equations or the incubator’s thermal parameters).
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in the Digital Twin (DT) system for the incubator involves real-time data transmission from the **Physical Twin (PT)** to the DT via **sensors** embedded in the system. Specifically, **three temperature sensors** (two inside the insulated styrofoam box and one outside) collect **analog temperature readings** and transmit them to the DT. The data types transmitted include **time-series temperature measurements** (likely in °C or Kelvin) with **high temporal resolution** to enable dynamic modeling and calibration. The communication protocol is not explicitly specified in the documents, but the system implies a **low-latency, bidirectional protocol** (e.g., **MQTT, Modbus, or OPC UA**) for real-time sensor data streaming. **Event triggers** for data transmission are likely **periodic polling** (e.g., every 1–10 seconds) or **asynchronous updates** when sensor thresholds are crossed. **Data quality assurance** is implied through **calibration algorithms** (mentioned in Document 1) that adjust the DT’s models (e.g., thermal dynamics) to match the PT’s behavior, ensuring accuracy. **Preprocessing steps** may include **filtering noise**, **normalization**, or **feature extraction** (e.g., temperature gradients) before integration into the DT’s **cyber-physical models**. Data integration mechanisms involve **fusion of sensor data** with **physical models** (e.g., differential equations for heat transfer) to generate a synchronized digital representation of the PT’s state.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in the incubator DT system involves **control commands** generated by the DT’s **algorithms** (e.g., supervisory controllers, optimizers) and transmitted to the **Physical Twin (PT)** to regulate its behavior. The **command types** include **setpoint adjustments** for the **heatbed** (e.g., PWM signals or duty-cycle modulation) and potentially **fan speed control** (if dynamically adjustable). The **control message format** is not explicitly detailed, but it likely follows a **structured protocol** (e.g., **Modbus TCP, CAN bus, or proprietary serial commands**) to ensure compatibility with the **Raspberry Pi controller**. **Validation and authentication mechanisms** are not described, but **safety checks** (e.g., bounds on temperature setpoints) are implied to prevent physical damage. **Feedback systems** operate via **closed-loop control**: the DT monitors the PT’s temperature sensors and adjusts commands iteratively to minimize error. **Conflict resolution** may involve **priority-based arbitration** (e.g., safety overrides for overheating) or **consensus algorithms** if multiple controllers are involved. **Command execution confirmation** is likely achieved via **acknowledgment signals** from the PT (e.g., Raspberry Pi confirming receipt and execution of commands).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **technical network infrastructure** for the incubator DT system is not explicitly detailed in the documents, but key inferences can be drawn. The system involves a **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** where the **Raspberry Pi** acts as the **edge controller**, interfacing between the PT’s sensors/actuators and the DT’s computational layer. The **communication protocols** are likely **lightweight and deterministic** (e.g., **MQTT for sensor data**, **Modbus/TCP for control commands**) to ensure low latency (<100ms) and reliability. The **network topology** is probably a **star architecture** with the Raspberry Pi as the central node, connecting to sensors/actuators via **I2C, SPI, or UART** interfaces. **Bandwidth requirements** are modest (e.g., <10 kbps for temperature data) but **latency must be minimized** (<1s) to support real-time calibration and control. **Reliability mechanisms** include **redundant sensor readings** (two internal temperature sensors) and **fail-safes** (e.g., hardware watchdogs on the Raspberry Pi). **Security protocols** are not specified but may involve **encryption for command transmission** (e.g., TLS for MQTT) and **access control** to prevent unauthorized DT modifications.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The **hosting and deployment infrastructure** for the incubator DT is implied to be **edge-centric** due to the system’s simplicity and real-time requirements. The **Raspberry Pi** serves as the **on-premise/edge hosting platform**, running the DT’s **models, calibration algorithms, and control logic** locally to minimize latency. **Computational requirements** are lightweight (e.g., solving low-order differential equations for thermal dynamics) but may scale with model complexity. **Storage requirements** are minimal, primarily for **historical sensor data** and **model parameters**. **Scalability mechanisms** are not explicitly discussed, but the modular design (e.g., separate models for cyber/physical components) suggests **plug-and-play extensibility** for additional sensors or actuators. **Deployment models** likely include **hybrid edge-cloud** (e.g., Raspberry Pi for real-time control, cloud for long-term analytics), though the documents focus on the **local DT implementation**. **Availability/reliability assurance** is achieved via **redundant hardware** (e.g., backup Raspberry Pi) and **fault-tolerant algorithms** (e.g., model fallback modes during sensor failures).
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the provided context includes the following phases: **(1) Modeling Phase**: Abstract representations of the Physical Twin (PT) are created at varying levels of abstraction (e.g., CAD models for geometric properties, dynamic models for physics-based behavior via differential equations). **(2) Integration Phase**: Models are integrated into the DT framework alongside real-time **data** (collected via sensors from the PT) and **algorithms** (e.g., calibration, fault detection, state estimation). **(3) Deployment Phase**: The DT is deployed alongside the PT, enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and optimization. **(4) Operational Phase**: The DT continuously updates models via **calibration algorithms** to reflect the PT’s current state, using feedback loops to adjust parameters (e.g., environmental or cyber-physical system (CPS) dynamics). **(5) Evolution Phase**: The DT adapts to changes in the PT or environment, with **reconfigurability** enabling robustness. Transition management between phases involves **calibration algorithms** that reconcile model parameters with real-world data, ensuring alignment between the DT and PT. Governance is implicit through **monitoring systems** that validate safe and optimal performance, triggering human or algorithmic interventions when deviations occur (e.g., via supervisory controllers).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process for the Digital Twin (DT) in the provided context follows a structured engineering methodology: **(1) System Analysis**: The Physical Twin (PT) is decomposed into its cyber and physical components (e.g., sensors, actuators, environmental interactions), with models built for each aspect (e.g., thermal dynamics of an incubator via differential equations). **(2) Model Development**: Models are created at multiple abstraction levels (e.g., geometric CAD models for structure, physics-based models for behavior), with a trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency (e.g., slower simulations for high-fidelity models). **(3) Data Integration**: Real-time sensor data from the PT (e.g., temperature sensors in the incubator case study) is ingested into the DT framework. **(4) Algorithm Implementation**: Algorithms are deployed for **calibration** (updating model parameters to match PT behavior), **monitoring** (safety/performance checks), and **control** (e.g., supervisory controllers for corrective actions). **(5) Validation**: Experimental results (e.g., Section 4 of the incubator case study) validate the DT’s accuracy and robustness, with **reconfigurability** enabling adaptation to environmental changes. **(6) Evolution Management**: The DT evolves via iterative calibration and model refinement, with version control implied through the **public repository** ([GitHub - INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)) hosting updated hardware/software specifications. Quality assurance is ensured through **experimental validation** (e.g., comparing DT predictions with PT behavior) and adherence to **systematic diagrams** (e.g., Fig. 1 for CPS-based DT structure).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin (DT) provides **decision-support capabilities** through the following mechanisms: **(1) Real-Time Insights**: The DT generates insights by processing **sensor data** (e.g., temperature readings from the incubator’s internal/external sensors) and **model outputs** (e.g., simulated thermal behavior under varying conditions). **(2) Analytics and Processing**: Algorithms perform **fault detection**, **state estimation** (e.g., estimating the PT’s internal temperature), and **optimization** (e.g., adjusting heater/fan settings to maintain target temperature). **(3) Decision-Making Processes**: Insights trigger **automated decisions** (e.g., supervisory controllers adjusting actuators) or **human-in-the-loop interventions** when violations (e.g., unsafe temperature deviations) are detected. **(4) User Interaction**: Users interact via **visualizations** (e.g., schematic overviews like Fig. 2) and **monitoring dashboards** (implied by the DT’s role in checking system performance). **(5) Confidence Measures**: The DT’s recommendations are grounded in **calibrated models** and **experimental validation** (e.g., Section 4’s results), with confidence derived from the alignment between simulated and real-world PT behavior. For example, the incubator DT’s decisions rely on **two temperature sensors** (internal) and **one external sensor**, cross-referenced with physics-based models to ensure accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin (DT) system for the incubator case study incorporates **safety-critical considerations** through its **monitoring and control mechanisms**, as depicted in Figure 1. The DT employs **calibration algorithms** to update models of the Cyber-Physical System (CPS) and environment, ensuring accurate parameter estimation for reliable operation. **Monitors** within the DT system actively check for **safe and optimal performance** of the physical twin (PT), detecting violations that may arise from deviations in operating conditions. While the documents do not explicitly detail **cybersecurity protocols** (e.g., encryption, access control, or authentication systems), the DT framework inherently relies on **real-time data integrity** from sensors (e.g., temperature sensors inside/outside the styrofoam box) and **fail-safe mechanisms** triggered by monitor-detected anomalies. The system’s **risk assessment** is implicit in its ability to predict and mitigate deviations via model-driven simulations, though no specific threat mitigation strategies (e.g., intrusion detection, secure communication protocols) are mentioned. The **safety-critical nature** of the incubator’s temperature regulation (e.g., ensuring stable conditions regardless of internal content) suggests adherence to **control-theoretic safety standards**, though no explicit compliance frameworks (e.g., ISO 26262, IEC 61508) are referenced.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.
The rest of the report is organized as follows: section 2 introduces the incubator


1 It is worth noting that models can be described at different levels of abstraction, and typically there is a
tendency that accurate models also are very slow to analyse for example by means of simulation.
2 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator)


2


Figure 1: Schematic overview of a CPS-based Digital Twins. The letter “C” and “P”
represents “Cyber” and “Physical” respectively.


system, including the hardware and software setup. Then we built two models with
calibration method in section 3. section 4 shows two kinds of experimental results.
Afterwards section 5 sketches the goals of the digital twinning and future work. Finally,
we summarise the report in section 6.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1
